CSE5313CSE5313 Coding and information theory for data science (Lecture 18)

CSE5313 Coding and information theory for data science (Lecture 18)

Secret sharing

The president and the vice president must both consent to a nuclear missile launch.

We would like to share the nuclear code such that:

  • Share1,Share2↦NuclearCodeShare1, Share2 \mapsto Nuclear CodeShare1,Share2↦NuclearCode
  • Share1↦̸NuclearCodeShare1 \not\mapsto Nuclear CodeShare1↦NuclearCode
  • Share2↦̸NuclearCodeShare2 \not\mapsto Nuclear CodeShare2↦NuclearCode
  • Share1↦̸Share2Share1 \not\mapsto Share2Share1↦Share2
  • Share2↦̸Share1Share2 \not\mapsto Share1Share2↦Share1

In other words:

  • The two shares are everything.
  • One share is nothing.

Solution

Scheme:

  • The nuclear code is a field element m∈Fqm \in \mathbb{F}_qm∈Fq​, chosen at random m∼Mm \sim Mm∼M (M arbitrary).
  • Let p(x)=m+rx∈Fq[x]p(x) = m + rx \in \mathbb{F}_q[x]p(x)=m+rx∈Fq​[x].
    • r∼Ur \sim Ur∼U, where U=UniformFqU = Uniform \mathbb{F}_qU=UniformFq​, i.e., Pr(α=1/q)Pr(\alpha = 1/q)Pr(α=1/q) for every α∈Fq\alpha \in \mathbb{F}_qα∈Fq​.
  • Fix α1,α2∈Fq\alpha_1, \alpha_2 \in \mathbb{F}_qα1​,α2​∈Fq​ (not random).
  • s1=p(α1)=m+rα1,s1∼S1s_1 = p(\alpha_1) = m + r\alpha_1, s_1 \sim S_1s1​=p(α1​)=m+rα1​,s1​∼S1​.
  • s2=p(α2)=m+rα2,s2∼S2s_2 = p(\alpha_2) = m + r\alpha_2, s_2 \sim S_2s2​=p(α2​)=m+rα2​,s2​∼S2​.

And then:

  • One share reveals nothing about mmm.
  • I.e., I(Si;M)=0I(S_i; M) = 0I(Si​;M)=0 (gradient could be anything)
  • Two shares reveal p⇒revealp(0)=mp \Rightarrow reveal p(0) = mp⇒revealp(0)=m.
  • I.e., H(M∣S1,S2)=0H(M|S_1, S_2) = 0H(M∣S1​,S2​)=0 (two points determine a line).

Formalize the notion of secret sharing

Problem setting

A dealer is given a secret mmm chosen from an arbitrary distribution MMM.

The dealer creates nnn shares s1,s2,⋯ ,sns_1, s_2, \cdots, s_ns1​,s2​,⋯,sn​ and send to nnn parties.

Two privacy parameters: t,z∈Nt,z\in \mathbb{N}t,z∈N z<tz<tz<t.

Requirements:

For A⊆[n]\mathcal{A}\subseteq[n]A⊆[n] denote SA={si:i∈A}S_\mathcal{A} = \{s_i:i\in \mathcal{A}\}SA​={si​:i∈A}.

  • Decodability: Any set of ttt shares can reconstruct the secret.
    • H(M∣ST)=0H(M|S_\mathcal{T}) = 0H(M∣ST​)=0 for all T⊆[n]\mathcal{T}\subseteq[n]T⊆[n] with ∣T∣≥t|\mathcal{T}|\geq t∣T∣≥t.
  • Security: Any set of zzz shares reveals no information about the secret.
    • I(M;SZ)=0I(M;S_\mathcal{Z}) = 0I(M;SZ​)=0 for all Z⊆[n]\mathcal{Z}\subseteq[n]Z⊆[n] with ∣Z∣≤z|\mathcal{Z}|\leq z∣Z∣≤z.

This is called (n,z,t)(n,z,t)(n,z,t)-secret sharing scheme.

Interpretation

  • Z⊆[n]\mathcal{Z} \subseteq [n]Z⊆[n], ∣Z∣≤z|\mathcal{Z}| \leq z∣Z∣≤z is a corrupted set of parties.
  • An adversary which corrupts at most zzz parties cannot infer anything about the secret.

Applications

  • Secure distributed storage.
    • Any ≤z\leq z≤z hacked servers reveal nothing about the data.
  • Secure distributed computing with a central server (e.g., federated learning).
    • Any ≤z\leq z≤z corrupted computation nodes know nothing about the data.
  • Secure multiparty computing (decentralized).
    • Any ≤z\leq z≤z corrupted parties cannot know the inputs of other parties.

Scheme 1: Shamir secret sharing scheme

Parameters n,tn,tn,t, and z=t−1z=t-1z=t−1.

Fix Fq\mathbb{F}_qFq​, q>nq>nq>n and distinct points α1,α2,⋯ ,αn∈Fq∖{0}\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \cdots, \alpha_n \in \mathbb{F}_q\setminus \{0\}α1​,α2​,⋯,αn​∈Fq​∖{0}. (public, known to all).

Given m∼Mm\sim Mm∼M the dealer:

  • Choose r1,r2,⋯ ,rz∼U1,U2,⋯ ,Uzr_1, r_2, \cdots, r_z \sim U_1, U_2, \cdots, U_zr1​,r2​,⋯,rz​∼U1​,U2​,⋯,Uz​ (uniformly random from Fq\mathbb{F}_qFq​).
  • Defines p∈Fq[x]p\in \mathbb{F}_q[x]p∈Fq​[x] by p(x)=m+r1x+r2x2+⋯+rzxzp(x) = m + r_1x + r_2x^2 + \cdots + r_zx^zp(x)=m+r1​x+r2​x2+⋯+rz​xz.
  • Send share si=p(αi)s_i = p(\alpha_i)si​=p(αi​) to party iii.

Theorem valid encoding scheme

This is an (n,t−1,t)(n,t-1,t)(n,t−1,t)-secret sharing scheme.

Decodability:

  • deg⁡p=t−1\deg p=t-1degp=t−1, any ttt shares can reconstruct ppp by Lagrange interpolation.

Proof

Specifically, any ttt parties T⊆[n]\mathcal{T}\subseteq[n]T⊆[n] can define the interpolation polynomial h(x)=∑i∈Tsiδi(x)h(x)=\sum_{i\in \mathcal{T}} s_i \delta_{i}(x)h(x)=∑i∈T​si​δi​(x), where δi(x)=∏j∈T∖{i}x−αjαi−αj\delta_{i}(x)=\prod_{j\in \mathcal{T}\setminus \{i\}} \frac{x-\alpha_j}{\alpha_i-\alpha_j}δi​(x)=∏j∈T∖{i}​αi​−αj​x−αj​​. (δi(αi)=1\delta_{i}(\alpha_i)=1δi​(αi​)=1, δi(αj)=0\delta_{i}(\alpha_j)=0δi​(αj​)=0 for j≠ij\neq ij=i).

deg⁡h=deg⁡p=t−1\deg h=\deg p=t-1degh=degp=t−1, so h(x)=p(x)h(x)=p(x)h(x)=p(x) for all x∈Tx\in \mathcal{T}x∈T.

Therefore, h(0)=p(0)=mh(0)=p(0)=mh(0)=p(0)=m.

Privacy:

Need to show that I(M;SZ)=0I(M;S_\mathcal{Z})=0I(M;SZ​)=0 for all Z⊆[n]\mathcal{Z}\subseteq[n]Z⊆[n] with ∣Z∣=z|\mathcal{Z}|=z∣Z∣=z.

that is equivalent to show that MMM and sZs_\mathcal{Z}sZ​ are independent for all Z⊆[n]\mathcal{Z}\subseteq[n]Z⊆[n] with ∣Z∣=z|\mathcal{Z}|=z∣Z∣=z.

Proof

We will show that Pr⁡(sZ∣M=m)=Pr⁡(M=m)\operatorname{Pr}(s_\mathcal{Z}|M=m)=\operatorname{Pr}(M=m)Pr(sZ​∣M=m)=Pr(M=m), for all sZ∈SZs_\mathcal{Z}\in S_\mathcal{Z}sZ​∈SZ​ and m∈Mm\in Mm∈M.

Let m,Z=(i1,i2,⋯ ,iz)m,\mathcal{Z}=(i_1,i_2,\cdots,i_z)m,Z=(i1​,i2​,⋯,iz​), and sZs_\mathcal{Z}sZ​.

[mU1U2⋯Uz]=[111⋯1αi1αi2αi3⋯αinαi12αi22αi32⋯αin2⋮⋮⋮⋱⋮αi1zαi2zαi3z⋯αinz]=sZ=[si1si2⋮siz]\begin{bmatrix} m & U_1 & U_2 & \cdots & U_z \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\ \alpha_{i_1} & \alpha_{i_2} & \alpha_{i_3} & \cdots & \alpha_{i_n} \\ \alpha_{i_1}^2 & \alpha_{i_2}^2 & \alpha_{i_3}^2 & \cdots & \alpha_{i_n}^2 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \alpha_{i_1}^{z} & \alpha_{i_2}^{z} & \alpha_{i_3}^{z} & \cdots & \alpha_{i_n}^{z} \end{bmatrix}=s_\mathcal{Z}=\begin{bmatrix} s_{i_1} \\ s_{i_2} \\ \vdots \\ s_{i_z} \end{bmatrix}[m​U1​​U2​​⋯​Uz​​]=​1αi1​​αi1​2​⋮αi1​z​​1αi2​​αi2​2​⋮αi2​z​​1αi3​​αi3​2​⋮αi3​z​​⋯⋯⋯⋱⋯​1αin​​αin​2​⋮αin​z​​​=sZ​=​si1​​si2​​⋮siz​​​​

So,

[U1U2⋯Uz]=(sZ−[mmm⋯m])[αi1−1αi2−1αi3−1⋯αin−1][111⋯1α1α2α3⋯αnα12α22α32⋯αn2⋮⋮⋮⋱⋮α1z−1α2z−1α3z−1⋯αnz−1]−1\begin{bmatrix} U_1 & U_2 & \cdots & U_z \end{bmatrix} = (s_\mathcal{Z}-\begin{bmatrix} m & m & m & \cdots & m \end{bmatrix}) \begin{bmatrix} \alpha_{i_1}^{-1} & \alpha_{i_2}^{-1} & \alpha_{i_3}^{-1} & \cdots & \alpha_{i_n}^{-1} \\ \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\ \alpha_1 & \alpha_2 & \alpha_3 & \cdots & \alpha_n \\ \alpha_1^2 & \alpha_2^2 & \alpha_3^2 & \cdots & \alpha_n^2 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \alpha_1^{z-1} & \alpha_2^{z-1} & \alpha_3^{z-1} & \cdots & \alpha_n^{z-1} \end{bmatrix}^{-1}[U1​​U2​​⋯​Uz​​]=(sZ​−[m​m​m​⋯​m​])[αi1​−1​​αi2​−1​​αi3​−1​​⋯​αin​−1​​]​1α1​α12​⋮α1z−1​​1α2​α22​⋮α2z−1​​1α3​α32​⋮α3z−1​​⋯⋯⋯⋱⋯​1αn​αn2​⋮αnz−1​​​−1

So exactly one solution for U1,U2,⋯ ,UzU_1, U_2, \cdots, U_zU1​,U2​,⋯,Uz​ is possible.

So Pr⁡(U1,U2,⋯ ,Uz∣M=m)=1qz\operatorname{Pr}(U_1, U_2, \cdots, U_z|M=m)=\frac{1}{q^z}Pr(U1​,U2​,⋯,Uz​∣M=m)=qz1​ for all m∈Mm\in Mm∈M.

Recall the law of total probability:

Pr⁡(sZ)=∑m′∈MPr⁡(sZ∣M=m′)Pr⁡(M=m′)=1qz∑m′∈MPr⁡(M=m′)=1qz\operatorname{Pr}(s_\mathcal{Z})=\sum_{m'\in M} \operatorname{Pr}(s_\mathcal{Z}|M=m') \operatorname{Pr}(M=m')=\frac{1}{q^z}\sum_{m'\in M} \operatorname{Pr}(M=m')=\frac{1}{q^z}Pr(sZ​)=m′∈M∑​Pr(sZ​∣M=m′)Pr(M=m′)=qz1​m′∈M∑​Pr(M=m′)=qz1​

So Pr⁡(sZ∣M=m)=Pr⁡(M=m)  ⟹  I(M;SZ)=0\operatorname{Pr}(s_\mathcal{Z}|M=m)=\operatorname{Pr}(M=m)\implies I(M;S_\mathcal{Z})=0Pr(sZ​∣M=m)=Pr(M=m)⟹I(M;SZ​)=0.

Scheme 2: Ramp secret sharing scheme (McEliece-Sarwate scheme)

  • Any zzz know nothing
  • Any ttt knows everything
  • Partial knowledge for z<s<tz<s<tz<s<t

Parameters n,tn,tn,t, and z<tz<tz<t.

Fix Fq\mathbb{F}_qFq​, q>nq>nq>n and distinct points α1,α2,⋯ ,αn∈Fq∖{0}\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \cdots, \alpha_n \in \mathbb{F}_q\setminus \{0\}α1​,α2​,⋯,αn​∈Fq​∖{0}. (public, known to all)

Given m1,m2,⋯ ,mn∼Mm_1, m_2, \cdots, m_n \sim Mm1​,m2​,⋯,mn​∼M, the dealer:

  • Choose r1,r2,⋯ ,rz∼U1,U2,⋯ ,Uzr_1, r_2, \cdots, r_z \sim U_1, U_2, \cdots, U_zr1​,r2​,⋯,rz​∼U1​,U2​,⋯,Uz​ (uniformly random from Fq\mathbb{F}_qFq​).
  • Defines p(x)=m1+m2x+⋯+mt−zxt−z−1+r1xt−z+r2xt−z+1+⋯+rzxt−1p(x) = m_1+m_2x + \cdots + m_{t-z}x^{t-z-1} + r_1x^{t-z} + r_2x^{t-z+1} + \cdots + r_zx^{t-1}p(x)=m1​+m2​x+⋯+mt−z​xt−z−1+r1​xt−z+r2​xt−z+1+⋯+rz​xt−1.
  • Send share si=p(αi)s_i = p(\alpha_i)si​=p(αi​) to party iii.

Decodability

Similar to Shamir scheme, any ttt shares can reconstruct ppp by Lagrange interpolation.

Privacy

Similar to the proof of Shamir, exactly one value of U1,⋯ ,UzU_1, \cdots, U_zU1​,⋯,Uz​ is possible!

Pr⁡(sZ∣m1,⋯ ,mt−z)=Pr⁡(U1,⋯ ,Uz)=theabove=1/qz\operatorname{Pr}(s_\mathcal{Z}|m_1, \cdots, m_{t-z}) = \operatorname{Pr}(U_1, \cdots, U_z) = the above = 1/q^zPr(sZ​∣m1​,⋯,mt−z​)=Pr(U1​,⋯,Uz​)=theabove=1/qz

(UiU_iUi​‘s are uniform and independent).

Conclude similarly by the law of total probability.

Pr⁡(sZ∣m1,⋯ ,mt−z)=Pr⁡(sZ)  ⟹  I(SZ;M1,⋯ ,Mt−z)=0\operatorname{Pr}(s_\mathcal{Z}|m_1, \cdots, m_{t-z}) = \operatorname{Pr}(s_\mathcal{Z}) \implies I(S_\mathcal{Z}; M_1, \cdots, M_{t-z}) = 0Pr(sZ​∣m1​,⋯,mt−z​)=Pr(sZ​)⟹I(SZ​;M1​,⋯,Mt−z​)=0.

Conditional mutual information

The dealer needs to communicate the shares to the parties.

Assumed: There exists a noiseless communication channel between the dealer and every party.

From previous lecture:

  • The optimal number of bits for communicating sis_isi​ (i’th share) to the i’th party is H(si)H(s_i)H(si​).
  • Q: What is H(si∣M)H(s_i|M)H(si​∣M)?

Tools:

  • Conditional mutual information.
  • Chain rule for mutual information.

Definition of conditional mutual information

The conditional mutual information I(X;Y∣Z)I(X;Y|Z)I(X;Y∣Z) of XXX and YYY given ZZZ is defined as:

I(X;Y∣Z)=H(X∣Z)−H(X∣Y,Z)=H(X∣Z)+H(X)−H(X)−H(X∣Y,Z)=(H(X)−H(X∣Y,Z))−(H(X)−H(X∣Z))=I(X;Y,Z)−I(X;Z)\begin{aligned} I(X;Y|Z)&=H(X|Z)-H(X|Y,Z)\\ &=H(X|Z)+H(X)-H(X)-H(X|Y,Z)\\ &=(H(X)-H(X|Y,Z))-(H(X)-H(X|Z))\\ &=I(X; Y,Z)- I(X; Z) \end{aligned}I(X;Y∣Z)​=H(X∣Z)−H(X∣Y,Z)=H(X∣Z)+H(X)−H(X)−H(X∣Y,Z)=(H(X)−H(X∣Y,Z))−(H(X)−H(X∣Z))=I(X;Y,Z)−I(X;Z)​

where H(X∣Y,Z)H(X|Y,Z)H(X∣Y,Z) is the conditional entropy of XXX given YYY and ZZZ.

The chain rule of mutual information

I(X;Y,Z)=I(X;Y∣Z)+I(X;Z)I(X;Y,Z)=I(X;Y|Z)+I(X;Z)I(X;Y,Z)=I(X;Y∣Z)+I(X;Z)

Conditioning reduces entropy.

Lower bound for communicating secret

Consider the Shamir scheme (z=t−1z = t - 1z=t−1, one message).

Q: What is H(si)H(s_i)H(si​) with respect to H(M)H(M)H(M) ? A: Fix any T={i1,⋯ ,it}⊆[n]\mathcal{T} = \{i_1, \cdots, i_t\} \subseteq [n]T={i1​,⋯,it​}⊆[n] of size ttt, and let Z={i1,⋯ ,it−1}\mathcal{Z} = \{i_1, \cdots, i_{t-1}\}Z={i1​,⋯,it−1​}.

H(M)=I(M;ST)+H(M∣ST)(by def. of mutual information)=I(M;ST)(since ST suffice to decode M)=I(M;Sit,SZ)(since ST=SZ∪Sit)=I(M;Sit∣SZ)+I(M;SZ)(chain rule)=I(M;Sit∣SZ)(since Z≤z, it reveals nothing about M)=I(Sit;M∣SZ)(symmetry of mutual information)=H(Sit∣SZ)−H(Sit∣M,SZ)(def. of conditional mutual information)≤H(Sit∣SZ)(entropy is non-negative)≤H(Sit∣SZ)(conditioning reduces entropy)\begin{aligned} H(M) &= I(M; S_\mathcal{T}) + H(M|S_\mathcal{T}) \text{(by def. of mutual information)}\\ &= I(M; S_\mathcal{T}) \text{(since }S_\mathcal{T}\text{ suffice to decode M)}\\ &= I(M; S_{i_t}, S_\mathcal{Z}) \text{(since }S_\mathcal{T} = S_\mathcal{Z} ∪ S_{i_t})\\ &= I(M; S_{i_t}|S_\mathcal{Z}) + I(M; S_\mathcal{Z}) \text{(chain rule)}\\ &= I(M; S_{i_t}|S_\mathcal{Z}) \text{(since }\mathcal{Z}\leq z \text{, it reveals nothing about M)}\\ &= I(S_{i_t}; M|S_\mathcal{Z}) \text{(symmetry of mutual information)}\\ &= H(S_{i_t}|S_\mathcal{Z}) - H(S_{i_t}|M,S_\mathcal{Z}) \text{(def. of conditional mutual information)}\\ &\leq H(S_{i_t}|S_\mathcal{Z}) \text{(entropy is non-negative)}\\ &\leq H(S_{i_t}|S_\mathcal{Z}) \text{(conditioning reduces entropy)} \\ \end{aligned}H(M)​=I(M;ST​)+H(M∣ST​)(by def. of mutual information)=I(M;ST​)(since ST​ suffice to decode M)=I(M;Sit​​,SZ​)(since ST​=SZ​∪Sit​​)=I(M;Sit​​∣SZ​)+I(M;SZ​)(chain rule)=I(M;Sit​​∣SZ​)(since Z≤z, it reveals nothing about M)=I(Sit​​;M∣SZ​)(symmetry of mutual information)=H(Sit​​∣SZ​)−H(Sit​​∣M,SZ​)(def. of conditional mutual information)≤H(Sit​​∣SZ​)(entropy is non-negative)≤H(Sit​​∣SZ​)(conditioning reduces entropy)​

So the bits used for sharing the secret is at least the bits of actual secret.

In Shamir we saw: H(si)≥H(M)H(s_i) \geq H(M)H(si​)≥H(M).

  • If MMM is uniform (standard assumption), then Shamir achieves this bound with equality.
  • In ramp secret sharing we have H(si)≥1t−zH(M1,⋯ ,Mt−z)H(s_i) \geq \frac{1}{t-z}H(M_1, \cdots, M_{t-z})H(si​)≥t−z1​H(M1​,⋯,Mt−z​) (similar proof).
  • Also optimal if MMM is uniform.

Downloading file with lower bandwidth from more servers
